prompt_text: How can a CFO or FP&A Lead at a Series B+ company build real-time financial models connected to live business data sources more efficiently?
response:
You get leverage by moving away from one-off spreadsheets and instead building a **driver-based model** on top of a **data hub** that auto-syncs from your ERP, billing, CRM, and HRIS, then pushing that into a modeling layer (Causal, Runway, Mosaic, Cube, or a spreadsheet + ETL) for scenarios and reporting.[1][2]

Below is a practical playbook oriented to a Series B+ CFO / FP&A lead.

---

## 1. Decide your target architecture

For a Series B+ startup (say **$3M–$20M+ ARR**), you typically want:

- A **single source of truth** for actuals (data warehouse or FP&A platform).[2]  
- A **driver-based forecasting model** that mirrors how the business really works.[1][3]  
- **Bi‑directional workflows**: ops systems → model (actuals, drivers) and model → stakeholders (dashboards, PDFs, board packs).[2]

Two common approaches:

| Approach | When it fits | Pros | Cons |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **All‑in‑one FP&A platform** (Runway, Causal, Mosaic, Cube, Jirav) | Finance team 2–10 FTE, multiple systems, need fast time to value | Native connectors, driver modeling, dashboards, less engineering | Less flexible than full warehouse; per-seat cost |
| **Spreadsheet + data stack** (Sheets/Excel + Fivetran/Airbyte + dbt + Looker/Mode) | Strong data team, complex product or pricing, high scale | Max flexibility, unify all data, reuse across org | Higher build/maintenance burden; longer to implement |

Given your question focuses on **efficiency** and **real-time**, most Series B+ teams will get the best ROI from an **FP&A platform with live integrations**, unless you already have a serious data engineering function.[2]

---

## 2. Pick the right tooling for your stage

Based on comparative reviews of FP&A tools for startups:[2]

- **$3M–$10M ARR (Series A–B)**  
  - **Runway**: stronger for **runway and headcount planning** with live sync from QBO/Xero, Stripe, Gusto/Rippling, payroll, etc.[2]  
  - **Causal**: stronger for **driver-based modeling & scenario analysis**, with live data feeds.[2]  
  - Recommended: **Runway for operational tracking**, **Causal for advanced modeling**.[2]

- **$10M+ ARR (Series C+)**  
  - **Mosaic** or **Cube** as an “enterprise FP&A” layer over your ERP, CRM, HRIS, billing.[2]  
  - **Mosaic**: full FP&A platform with dashboards, workflows, variance analysis, and headcount planning.[2]  
  - **Cube**: lets you keep Excel/Sheets as the front-end but automates data sync and collaboration.[2]  

- **Jirav** is another mid-market option for integrated forecasting with QuickBooks/NetSuite, Salesforce, HRIS integrations.[2]

If you want to stay in spreadsheets but gain real-time data, tools like **Cube** or building your own ETL → Google Sheets pipeline is the middle path.[2]

---

## 3. Integrate the right data sources

At Series B+, aim to connect at least:

- **ERP / GL**: QuickBooks Online, Xero, NetSuite – P&L, BS, CF actuals.[2]  
- **Billing / Payments**: Stripe, Chargebee, Recurly, custom billing – ARR, MRR, churn, expansions.[2]  
- **CRM**: Salesforce, HubSpot – pipeline, win rates, sales cycle, ACV.[2][3]  
- **HRIS / Payroll**: Gusto, Rippling, Justworks, Deel, etc. – current headcount, comp, benefits.[2]  
- **Product / Ops systems** (for complex or B2B2C models): usage, deployments, seats, transactions.[3]

Use the FP&A tool’s native connectors (Runway, Causal, Mosaic, Jirav, Cube) so **actuals refresh automatically** instead of CSV uploads.[2]  
This is what converts your model from “monthly close” to *near real-time*.

---

## 4. Design a driver-based model once, not every quarter

To make your real‑time data useful, the model needs to be **driver-based** and **modular**.[1][3]

### Core design principles

- **Start with data, not guesses**: anchor assumptions in historicals and benchmark metrics like CAC, churn, retention, and growth rates for similar companies.[1][4]
